question: Does the paper report in vitro drug susceptibility data?
evidence: We then measured the effects of these mutations in isolation or with R263K on the relative infectiousness of NL4.3 virus as described previously (5). HIV-1 infectivity was measured through the infection of 30,000 TZM-bl cells per well using serial 1:4 dilutions of the NL4.3 variants. After 48 h, cells were lysed, and luciferase production was measured using the Promega luciferase assay system (Promega, Madison, WI). Each single substitution had a negative impact on infectiousness. The NL4.3IN(Y143RR263K) and NL4.3IN(Q148RR263K) viruses showed the greatest declines with increases in relative 50% effective concentration (EC50) of 17.9- and 29.6-fold compared to the wild type, respectively. The NL4.3IN(N155HR263K) virus had a significantly lower EC50 than did the NL4.3IN(R263K) single mutant (fold change of 1.2 for N155HR263K compared to 2.7 for R263K alone), suggesting that the addition of N155H partially restored the deficit in infectivity observed with R263K.
rationale: The paper mentions measuring HIV-1 susceptibility to DTG, RAL, and EVG in tissue culture through the determination of the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50). This indicates that in vitro drug susceptibility data was reported.

question: Which HIV species were studied in the paper?
evidence: Although considerable progress has been made in HIV therapy, drug resistance has emerged for every available drug class, threatening long-term care and the potential for a cure (1). The first two integrase strand transfer inhibitors (INSTIs), raltegravir (RAL) and elvitegravir (EVG), can be compromised by primary substitutions that generate resistance at the expense of fitness (2), the majority of which occur in the catalytic core domain of the integrase protein (IN). Although the newer INSTI dolutegravir (DTG) has not yielded resistance when used in initial therapy (3), our group selected an R263K substitution in HIV-1 IN in tissue culture with DTG (4).
rationale: The paper mentions the study of HIV-1.
